headline: CATTLE RUNS HARD, WHEAT HOLDS GAINS INTO WEDNESDAY
subheadline: Live cattle's 2.4% overnight move leads the complex as screwworm cases climb and the wheat rally from Tuesday consolidates.
lead: Live cattle at $249.12 is the story this morning, a 2.4% overnight run that puts the contract within range of last week's highs one day after the Cargill Fort Morgan lockout, ongoing since May 19, had the complex looking like it was starting to crack. Screwworm cases climbing to 12 is the catalyst the market needed to separate supply anxiety from processing disruption anxiety. Wheat at $6.14 is holding most of Tuesday's 4% run; the question this week was whether USDA Crop Progress would confirm the Belt caught up to seasonal pace, and Monday's 3 PM report gave you the answer. Now the market is repricing what that planting pace means for the rest of the summer.
the_takeaway: Cattle found its bid; screwworm supply pressure is doing what lockout processing pressure couldn't.
teaser: Cattle runs 2.4% on screwworm pressure, wheat holds big gains, corn quietly adds a cent.

one_number.context: The screwworm case count climbing to 12 is not just a biosecurity headline. Each confirmed case tightens the cattle supply side independently of the Cargill Fort Morgan lockout, which constrains processing capacity. Two separate pressures on the same complex at the same time explains why live cattle ran 2.4% overnight rather than drifting. Watch whether the case count crosses 20 before month-end; that is the threshold where USDA typically escalates movement restrictions.

yesterdays_call.summary: Yesterday's high-conviction call: wheat had its day; corn needs the weather pattern to clarify before it follows.
yesterdays_call.note: Wheat is holding $6.14 and corn added a modest cent at $4.17, exactly the split the call described.

sections.title: CATTLE RUNS ON SCREWWORM
sections.body: Live cattle at **$249.12**, up 2.4% overnight, is the sharpest single-session move in the complex in weeks, and the driver is not the Cargill Fort Morgan lockout but the screwworm case count climbing to 12. That matters because the lockout constrains processing throughput; screwworm constrains the supply pipeline itself. Two different pressure points, same direction. Feeder cattle at **$367.62** added 1.7%, which tells you the market is reading the screwworm risk as a broader cattle supply problem, not just a border-state nuisance. The Cargill situation, with roughly 2% of weekly U.S. slaughter capacity still offline, remains unresolved and adds a processing floor to a supply story that is now running on its own legs. May retail fresh meat inflation data published this morning shows prolonged price pressure testing consumer resilience, which caps the upside but does not remove it.
sections.catalyst: Screwworm cases climb to 12; Cargill Fort Morgan lockout unresolved at 6,000 head/day offline.
sections.bottom_line: Supply pressure and processing disruption are pulling the same direction; the bid is real.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: Monday's recovery call confirmed; screwworm drove the acceleration.

sections.title: WHEAT HOLDS, CORN WAITS
sections.body: Wheat at **$6.14** is up another 9.5 cents today after Tuesday's 4% run, holding the bulk of that move without giving it back. That is the market telling you Tuesday's rally had real buying behind it, not just short covering. Corn at **$4.17** added 4.25 cents, a quiet 1% that follows along without leading. The 2026 planting season, behind the average through May before catching up between rain systems, is now well-documented in the Crop Progress data, and atmospheric scientist Eric Snodgrass is flagging an El Nino transition and elevated soil moisture as the top weather concerns for the rest of June. Elevated soil moisture in key Belt districts is not a yield drag today, but it sets up a late-June story if El Nino dries the pattern faster than the models are projecting. December corn at **$4.46** added the same 4.25 cents as nearby, meaning no change in the carry structure. The market is not pricing weather premium yet, but it has the calendar circled.
sections.catalyst: Snodgrass flags El Nino transition and 150%-of-normal soil moisture as June weather risks.
sections.bottom_line: Wheat held its run; corn is tagging along, waiting for a weather catalyst that is not here yet.
sections.vs_yesterday: Wheat: held gains after the big session, no significant reversion.

sections.title: SOYBEANS STEADY, INPUTS EASE
sections.body: Soybeans at **$11.57** added a half-cent, continuing the quiet consolidation after Tuesday's follow-through. China's $17 billion annual U.S. ag purchase commitment through 2028, announced May 18, has not translated into fresh sustained futures support beyond the initial bounce, and there is no new export catalyst in today's news bucket. Soybean oil at **$73.20** added 1.0% and soybean meal at **$308.40** added 0.4%, so the crush complex is holding but not expanding its margin. The more interesting input story today is on the nitrogen side: urea prices at New Orleans tumbled Monday on U.S.-Iran preliminary peace deal news. The Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, are working their way through the fertilizer complex as Iranian crude exports reportedly resumed past the U.S. blockade this morning. Producers watching fall fertilizer costs have a real window here. Lock pre-pay nitrogen now if your cash flow allows; the peace-deal premium is exiting the market faster than the geopolitical risk is being repriced.
sections.catalyst: Iran crude exports resuming; urea prices at New Orleans tumbled Monday on peace deal progress.
sections.bottom_line: Beans are quiet; the fertilizer input story is louder than the futures board today.
sections.farmer_action: If pre-pay nitrogen is available at your co-op, get a quote this week. Urea is moving lower on the Iran deal and the window may not stay open past month-end.

sections.title: CRUDE STEADIES AFTER TUESDAY BREAK
sections.body: WTI crude at **$75.85** added 1.4% on the day, a partial recovery after Tuesday's hard drop on Iran deal news. The first Iranian crude oil exports in two months moved past the U.S. blockade outside the Strait this morning, which tells you the market is reading the diplomatic channel as real, not just rumor. Natural gas at **$3.29** added 1.4% as well, partly on the Australian Ichthys LNG strike resolution and Qatar preparing to return LNG tankers ahead of the Hormuz reopening. For U.S. grain producers, the diesel input story is more relevant than crude itself: the biofuels sector is flagging confidence in meeting higher EPA blending requirements finalized in March, which supports domestic crush demand for soybean oil and gives the renewable diesel complex a structural floor.
sections.catalyst: First Iranian crude exports resume post-blockade; EPA higher blending volumes supporting biofuels demand.
sections.bottom_line: Crude stabilizing, not recovering; fertilizer and diesel costs are the ag-relevant story here.

outside_the_pit.title: El Nino Transition Flagged as June's Top Weather Risk
outside_the_pit.body: Atmospheric scientist Eric Snodgrass says the switch toward El Nino and soil moisture running 150% of normal in key Belt districts are his top concerns for the rest of June. Neither is in the futures price today, but pollination timing in corn is approaching, and El Nino's pattern shift could flip the soil-moisture story from drag to drought risk faster than models currently suggest.

outside_the_pit.title: Brazil's 2027 Corn and Soy Expansion Still USDA's Base Case
outside_the_pit.body: Despite low prices, tight credit, and rising fertilizer costs, USDA's published forecast calls for continued record Brazilian corn and soybean production in 2027 driven by area expansion and yield gains. U.S. producers competing for the same export market share should treat Brazil's supply trajectory as a structural headwind that no single China deal fully offsets.

outside_the_pit.title: Denmark Scraps Agriculture Minister Role
outside_the_pit.body: Denmark's new government eliminated the agriculture minister position, splitting traditional ag responsibilities into a nature and animal welfare ministry. Denmark is a top EU pork and dairy exporter, and the institutional shift signals a regulatory reorientation that could reshape European supply-side policy over the next few years, with downstream implications for U.S. protein exports competing in EU markets.

spread_to_watch.label: Live cattle / feeder cattle ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: Live cattle at $249.12 and feeders at $367.62 put the ratio at roughly 0.678, and both contracts are running higher together, which is the market pricing supply pressure on both ends of the chain simultaneously. When screwworm cases and the lockout are both active, the ratio normally widens as feeders get hit harder than fats; the fact that it is holding narrow tells you the live side is catching up, not that feeders are losing their bid.

basis.headline: Corn basis firming in the eastern Belt; wheat basis mixed post-rally.
basis.body: **Eastern Belt corn basis is tightening** as ethanol grind continues at pace and old-crop storage positions start to thin ahead of harvest. Producers east of the Mississippi with old-crop bushels in the bin have a basis window the futures board at $4.17 alone is not fully reflecting. Western Belt corn basis is staying soft, no change from the seasonal pattern. **Wheat basis is mixed** in the wake of Tuesday's big session, with elevators resetting bids cautiously while they watch whether $6.14 holds or pulls back.

weekly_thread.question: Will USDA Crop Progress confirm the Belt caught up to seasonal pace after May's wet start?
weekly_thread.status_text: Monday's Crop Progress confirmed the Belt is on pace, and the market has moved on to the next question: what does an El Nino transition mean for a crop planted into 150%-of-normal soil moisture. The planting question is answered; the growing-season weather question is now opening.

the_more_you_know.title: The Fertilizer Price Window That Opens When Crude Falls
the_more_you_know.body: Urea at New Orleans dropped sharply Monday on U.S.-Iran peace deal news, and this morning Iranian crude exports reportedly resumed for the first time in two months. That connection is not coincidental: roughly 30% of global nitrogen fertilizer production is natural-gas-intensive, and when Middle East supply risk premiums exit the energy complex, they exit the nitrogen complex within days, not weeks. The $75.85 WTI print today is already $9 below last month's peak, and urea typically follows crude's direction with a 1-to-3 week lag. Producers who pre-pay fall nitrogen in this window are effectively locking in a peace-deal discount that may not survive if diplomatic progress stalls or Iranian export volumes disappoint. The window is real, it is probably 2 to 4 weeks wide, and it is already open.

watch_list.desc: USDA Weekly Export Sales: soybean sales below 300,000 MT keeps the chart range-bound; above 450,000 MT gives the bulls a fresh catalyst heading into weekend weather coverage.
Screwworm case count: if confirmed U.S. cases cross 20, watch for USDA to escalate movement restrictions, which would tighten the feeder supply side independently of the Cargill lockout.
Iranian crude export volumes: if daily flows through the Strait of Hormuz reach pre-blockade pace, the fertilizer input window narrows. Get nitrogen quotes before that repricing happens.
Cattle: if live cattle holds above $248 into Friday's close with the lockout still unresolved, the market is telling you screwworm supply pressure is carrying more weight than processing capacity.

daily_quote.text: He who knows when he can fight and when he cannot, will be victorious.

source_summary: USDA NASS Crop Progress, CME Group settlement prices, NYMEX WTI, Brownfield Ag News, Beef Magazine, Farm Doc Daily, OilPrice.com, EIA, Farm Policy News
